coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of Fe3O4@graphite sheets as electrocatalyst for water electrolysis
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Immanuel Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar
Perumal, Suguna
Vinodh, Rajangam
Muthuchamy, Nallal
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: In this study, Fe3O4@graphite sheets were successfully synthesized in one-pot via a simple hydrothermal method and subsequently employed as an effective electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). HER is the crucial electrochemical reactions for the next-generation energy devices. Hydrogen is a clean and renewable energy resource and is one of the most promising candidates for fuel. The shape of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles is nearly spherical with a size of about 10 nm and the Fe3O4 nanoparticles distributed evenly on the graphite sheets. The synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets have a specific surface area about 125 m2 g−1 with slit-shaped open micro/mesopores. Overall the physicochemical characterization techniques have confirmed the formation, structural stability, and elemental composition of synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets. The resulting Fe3O4@graphite sheets were demonstrated towards electrocatalytic HER in 0.1 M KOH solution. The synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets delivered the lowest Tafel slope (78 mV dec–1) and overpotential (–120 mVRHE) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results demonstrate that HER are highly favorable on the Fe3O4@graphite sheets due to the relatively low charge transfer resistance. The experimental results reveal that Fe3O4@graphite sheets possess remarkable electrocatalytic HER activities and are highly stable. The good electrocatalytic activity of Fe3O4@graphite sheet can be ascribed to the synergistic effect between Fe3O4 and graphite sheets, which can facilitate charge transfer kinetics to enhance electrocatalytic HER performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3O4 nanoparticle
Graphite sheet
Water splitting
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Renewable fuel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korean funded by the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675), the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) (2017R1C1B5076345) and the MSIT (2018R1A2B2004432).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of Fe3O4@graphite sheets with different magnification.
(a) FESEM image of Fe3O4@graphite sheets and corresponding elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, and (d) iron. (e) EDX spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets.
(a) XRD patterns of Fe3O4@graphite sheets and pure graphite powder and (b) ATR-FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets. High-resolution XPS of (b) C 1 s level, (c) O 1 s level, and (d) Fe 2p level for Fe3O4@graphite sheets.

Electrochemical performance (a) linear sweep cyclic voltammetry curves and (b) Tafel slopes of the bare Pt plate and Fe3O4@graphite sheets. (c) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of Fe3O4@graphite sheets with different overpotential (inset (c): Nyquist plot is fitted with a suitable equivalent electric circuit diagram) and (d) stability of Fe3O4@graphite sheets at a constant current density of –10 mA cm−2 (inset (d) is LSV curves before and after prolong the stability of Fe3O4@graphite sheets) towards HER.
One-pot synthesis of Fe3O4@graphite sheets via a simple hydrothermal method.
Comparison of graphene-based metal oxides for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of Fe3O4@graphite sheets as electrocatalyst for water electrolysis

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, Fe3O4@graphite sheets were successfully synthesized in one-pot via a simple hydrothermal method and subsequently employed as an effective electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). HER is the crucial electrochemical reactions for the next-generation energy devices. Hydrogen is a clean and renewable energy resource and is one of the most promising candidates for fuel. The shape of the Fe3O4 nanoparticles is nearly spherical with a size of about 10 nm and the Fe3O4 nanoparticles distributed evenly on the graphite sheets. The synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets have a specific surface area about 125 m2 g−1 with slit-shaped open micro/mesopores. Overall the physicochemical characterization techniques have confirmed the formation, structural stability, and elemental composition of synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets. The resulting Fe3O4@graphite sheets were demonstrated towards electrocatalytic HER in 0.1 M KOH solution. The synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets delivered the lowest Tafel slope (78 mV dec–1) and overpotential (–120 mVRHE) at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy results demonstrate that HER are highly favorable on the Fe3O4@graphite sheets due to the relatively low charge transfer resistance. The experimental results reveal that Fe3O4@graphite sheets possess remarkable electrocatalytic HER activities and are highly stable. The good electrocatalytic activity of Fe3O4@graphite sheet can be ascribed to the synergistic effect between Fe3O4 and graphite sheets, which can facilitate charge transfer kinetics to enhance electrocatalytic HER performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The energy resources available currently are insufficient to fulfill our needs because of their rapid reduction of availability. Thus substitute energy sources are highly demanded. Among the energy sources, hydrogen (H2) is a possible energy carrier for the cost-cutting measure meant for sustainable, clean, storable, and environmental-friendly energy [1–6]. Generally, coal, oil, and natural gas are considered sources of hydrogen, decomposition of such fossil fuels results in hydrogen gas. However, decomposition gives rise to environmental pollution with the emission of carbon dioxide (CO2) gas which causes global warming. Thus indeed the replacement of fossil fuels is urgently needed. Recently many researchers focus on the production of H2 gas by electrocatalytic water splitting [7–13]. This electrocatalytic water splitting is a simple and efficient technology, which requires electrocatalysts to lower the overpotential of the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [14]. For this platinum (Pt) is an excellent electrocatalyst for the HER. However, Pt is a highly expensive one and low abundantly available in nature [15,16].

In recent years, functional electrocatalytic materials such as carbon-based materials, conducting polymers, transition metal chalcogenides, transition metal nitrides, and carbides are used for the production of hydrogen gas by the electrocatalytic method [17–21]. In the carbon-based materials, graphene composed materials attracted great attention. This is because of the unique electrochemical behaviors of graphene materials. Lately, carbon with transition metal-based composites was used in the replacement of platinum [22–24]. Usually, carbon materials prepared and they are decorated/combined with transition metals to prepare composites [25–27]. Among the transition metal oxides, iron oxide (Fe3O4) exhibits great potential for the wide range of applications such as energy, environmental and biomedical field due to its low cost, relatively low toxicity, and environmental friendliness [28]. Considerable effort has been expended on the synthesis of composite materials based on Fe3O4 with graphene/carbon [29]. These Fe3O4 with graphene/carbon composite possess large specific surface areas with open pores, strong magnetic dipole interaction between Fe3O4 nanoparticles which is responsible for the high chemical stability [14]. Also, it provides enough space for the diffusion of the electrolyte to the active sites of the electrode and will make a positive contribution to its electrochemical performance [30]. These methods need additional time to overcome these disadvantages.

Here, we describe the one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of Fe3O4@graphite sheets. Iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles were decorated on graphite sheets. The prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets were well characterized using various physicochemical techniques. Besides, the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets were employed for the HER. The electrochemical measurements of synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets for HER were carried out through a three-electrode cell system in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolytes at atmospheric conditions.

Iron (III) acetylacetonate (Fe(III) acac), graphite powder, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), potassium hydroxide (KOH), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP), benzyl alcohol (BA), ethyl alcohol (EtOH) and dichloromethane (DCM) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The carbon cloth (CC) electrode was received from FuelCellStore, USA. All of the chemicals were used as received and deionized water was used throughout this work.

Inside the glovebox, Iron (III) acetylacetonate (1.0 g) and graphite powder (0.5 g) in 40 mL benzyl alcohol were added into a 100 mL Teflon lined autoclave. Followed by the hydrothermal reaction was carried out in the autoclave by heating at 180 °C for 48 h in an oven and then chilled to normal temperature [31,32]. The obtained mixture was centrifuged, washed with ethanol, and DCM. Then the solid product was dried at 70 °C for 5 h. The resultant solid product (Fe3O4@graphite sheets) was investigated thoroughly and stored under argon for further use. The illustration of the synthesis procedure of Fe3O4@graphite sheets is shown in Scheme 1 .

Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis of Insitu iron oxide (Fe3O4) nanoparticles embedded graphite sheets (Fe3O4@graphite sheets) by a simple hydrothermal method. The prepared composites (Fe3O4@graphite sheets) were extensively characterized. Fig. 1 (a–d) shows the FESEM images of Fe3O4@graphite sheets with different magnification. From Fig. 1 it is very clear about the irregular shape sheets of graphite. Fig. 1(c & d) reveals that iron oxide nanoparticles distributed evenly on the graphite sheets. The shape of the iron oxide nanoparticles are nearly spherical and the size of the nanoparticles was measured about 10 nm. Fig. 2 shows the FESEM image and elemental mapping of Fe3O4@graphite sheets. The elemental mapping confirms the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and iron (Fe). The C element is from the graphite sheets, O and Fe elements confirms the presence of iron oxide nanoparticles (some of O element is from exfoliated graphite sheets during the hydrothermal process). O and Fe elements were evenly distributed on the graphite sheets which are clear from the mapping images. Further, the EDX confirms the existence of the elements C, O, and Fe. FESEM analysis reveals the existence of elements, O and Fe on the graphite sheets.

XRD analysis is a powerful tool to explain the crystallinity and to check the size of the nanoparticles. Fig. 3 (a) shows the XRD patterns of pure graphite powder and Fe3O4@graphite sheets. A sharp and strong diffraction peak around 2θ = 26.5° (0 0 2) confirms the presence of graphitic materials [33,34]. The intensity of the peak around 2θ = 26.1° (0 0 2) decreased and slightly shifted towards lower 2θ for Fe3O4@graphite sheets suggested that graphene sheets from graphite powder might partially be exfoliated due to hydrothermal reaction. Additionally, characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (inset Fig. 3(a)) were observed at 30.4 (2 2 0), 35.7 (3 1 1), 43.4 (4 0 0), 53.7 (4 2 2), 57.3 (5 1 1) and 63.0° (4 4 0) which are well agreed with the inverse cubic spinel phase of Fe3O4 (magnetite, JCPS card no. 88–0315) [32,35–37]. Using XRD pattern the mean crystallite diameter was calculated as 10 nm for Fe3O4 particles based on the (3 1 1) plane by using Scherrer’s formula. This shows consistent results with the size of the particles calculated using FESEM measurements. Raman spectroscopy studies were used to confirm the composition of Fe3O4@graphite sheets and the degree of graphitization of graphite sheets. The Raman spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets is depicted as Fig. S1. An intense broad peak can be noticed around ~ 681 cm−1 which is attributed to the A1g mode of the symmetric stretch of oxygen atoms along with Fe (Fe − O bonds) [38]. Additionally, five weak peaks at about 122, 220, 292, 360, and 515 cm−1 are associated with the T2g vibration modes of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [31,39]. Further, three major peaks around 1346, 1586, and 2724 cm−1 resemble the carbon phase of D-band, G-band, and 2D-band, respectively [40–44]. The low intensity of the D-band (ID) is associated with lesser defects in the sp2 carbon lattice. This suggests the high degree of graphitization of the graphite sheet in the composite of Fe3O4@graphite sheets. Besides, the area of D-band (AD) and G-band (AG) represents the concentration of defects in the graphitic structure, and the lower AD in the Raman spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets implies the good graphitization of resulting graphite sheets [45].

FTIR spectroscopy was used to study the functionalization of the graphite and Fe3O4@graphite sheets. Fig. S2 shows the FTIR spectrum of graphite powder since it does not support a static dipole moment, characteristic peaks were not observed [46] hence broad hump appeared between 500 and 1500 cm−1 which might be due to the framework vibrations of graphite. Thus the observed absorption peaks of Fe3O4@graphite sheets were mainly from Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 3 (b)). Broadband around 632 cm−1 is attributed to the Fe–O vibration of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles [47]. The characteristic bands of C–H, C = O, C–OH, and C–O–C stretching vibrations appeared at 2852, 1724, 1406, and 1220 cm−1, respectively [39,48]. The C = C stretching vibration observed at 1551 cm−1 [49,50], and the C–H bending vibration observed at 872 cm−1 [51]. The results confirm the existence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on graphene sheets. The surface chemical composition of Fe3O4@graphite sheets was further analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum of the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets exhibits the presence of C, O, and Fe elements (Fig. 4 (a)). Fig. 4 (b) shows C 1 s spectra of Fe3O4@graphite sheets contained three typical components, corresponding to the carbon atoms in three types of functional groups. The nonoxygenated ring C = C/C–C (284.4 eV), the (oxygenated carbon) C in C–OH bond (285.6 eV), and the C in C = O (289.3 eV) [31,52–56]. Fig. 4 (c) shows the O 1 s spectrum of Fe3O4@graphite sheets which consists of three peaks. The oxygen that binds to iron (Fe–O) detected at 529.6 eV, oxygen in hydroxyl (C-OH) detected at 530.8 eV, and oxygen in carbonyl (C = O) observed at 532.5 eV [31,57–60]. As shown in Fig. 4 (d), the XPS peaks of Fe 2p spectra located at 710.5 eV and 724 eV, which can be assigned to Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively of Fe3+ ion in the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets [61–64]. Besides, a satellite peak appeared at 718 eV. The deconvolution of the Fe 2p peaks showed four peaks. Fe 2p3/2 peak deconvoluted into two peaks centered at 710.0 eV and 711.8 eV, Fe 2p1/2 deconvoluted into two peaks which are centered at 723.7 eV and 725.5 eV. The XPS measurements confirm the functional groups and compositions of synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets, that results are consistent with ATR-FTIR spectroscopy.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets were examined using the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms as shown in Fig. S3. The sorption isotherms exhibit cross-curves between type-I and type-IV behavior with a combination of the H1 and H3 hysteresis loop (according to the IUPAC classification), demonstrating the presence of both micropores and mesopores in the synthesized solid material [65,66]. The competitive capillary condensation step at low relative pressures (P/P0, ~0.0–0.15) is associated with smaller micropores. The sharp capillary condensation step at high relative pressures (P/P0, ~0.8–0.95) are associated with large pores with a narrow mesopore size distribution. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area was measured as 125 m2 g−1 for the synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets. Furthermore, the pore-size distribution (inset of Fig. S3) was obtained from the desorption branch of the isotherms by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method which reveals that the Fe3O4@graphite sheets might have slit-shaped open micro/mesopores. Besides, the pore size analysis model gave a large proportion of mesopores with a narrow pore size distribution. The pore diameter was measured in the range 3.5–4.5 nm with a peak pore diameter of approximately 3.8 nm, suggesting the uniform distribution of Fe3O4 nanoparticles on the graphite sheets [67,68]. The slit-shaped open mesopores observed in the Fe3O4@graphite sheets are believed to be beneficial for the diffusion of the electrolyte to the active sites of the electrode and this will make a positive contribution to its electrochemical performance [65].

The electrocatalytic activity of bare Pt plate and Fe3O4@graphite sheets toward HER was assessed by the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) performance using a three-electrode cell setup. The cell arrangement was done in a 0.1 M KOH solution at 10 mV s−1. LSV and Tafel slope of the bare Pt plate and Fe3O4@graphite sheets are revealed in Fig. 5 . The potentials obtained were standardized to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale. The Fe3O4@graphite sheets exhibit good electroactivity with high current density, and lower overpotential (–120 mVRHE). The cathodic current density is directly associated with the amount of hydrogen evolved. The superior electrocatalytic performance of Fe3O4@graphite sheets is attributed to the graphite sheets with Fe3O4 nanoparticles. This is accountable for the electron pairing/transport from Fe3O4 nanoparticles to electrodes. In contrast, graphite sheets which have large surface area are responsible for the good dispersion of Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Also, it shows an optimistic effect between the graphite sheets and Fe3O4 nanoparticles. The Tafel polarization investigation was performed to understand the HER mechanism. The Tafel plots of bare Pt plate and Fe3O4@graphite sheets derived from the polarization curves are displayed as Fig. 5 (b). The plots were obtained by plotting the logarithmic values of the current density against overpotential. The Tafel slope for the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets was calculated as 78 mV dec–1. This slope is acceptably closer as that of bare Pt (40 mV dec–1) which confirms the better catalytic activity of prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets [4,6,69]. This is a shred of evidence that the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets have high cathodic current density and lower Tafel slopes. The synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets exhibit competitive electrocatalytic performance compared to current graphene-based catalytic materials which are presented in Table 1
[6,12,16,21,23,70,71]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is used to investigate the interface reactions, electrode kinetics of contact, and charge-transfer impedance during the HER. This proves superior electrochemical activity with minor impedance. The charge-transfer resistance (Rct) is correlated to the electrocatalytic kinetics, lower the charge-transfer resistance value, faster the reaction rate. EIS measurements of Fe3O4@graphite sheets were performed at the different overpotentials (such as –36, –136, –236, and –736 mVRHE) perturbation of 10 mV s−1 of amplitude from the frequency range of 10 mHz to 10 kHz (Fig. 5(c)). The Nyquist plot is fitted with an equivalent electric circuit (R1 + Q2/R2) using Z-View software (inset of Fig. 5(c)). The EIS values were surprisingly decreased with increasing the overpotential values. It advises that Rct decreases with increasing negative (–ve) electrode potentials [72]. Further, the long-term stability of the prepared Fe3O4@graphite sheets is also analyzed in an alkaline environment. Long-term potential cycling at constant potentials value for a longer time constant current density of –10 mA cm−2 were performed and the results are shown in Fig. 5(d). The potential of HER electrocatalytic activity for Fe3O4@graphite sheets remains unchanged above 43200 s. The LSV measurements before prolonging stability (initial) and after prolong stability (final) were compared (inset of Fig. 5(d)). Insignificant changes of LSV values were observed which confirm the excellent stability of synthesized Fe3O4@graphite sheets towards HER under alkaline medium [73].

In summary, we explored a one-pot synthesis of novel Fe3O4@graphite sheets via a simple hydrothermal method and were employed as an electrocatalyst for HER under alkaline environment. Fe3O4 nanoparticles were consistently embedded on the graphite sheets and which make more active sites for electrocatalytic reaction. When the Fe3O4@graphite sheets as the catalyst for HER, the electrodes present enhanced electrocatalytic activity with the overpotential of –120 mVRHE and the lower Tafel slope of 78 mV dec–1. Moreover, the excellent long-term stability of Fe3O4@graphite sheets can be obtained. The outstanding activity for HER with the preeminent long-term stability of Fe3O4@graphite sheets is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect between graphite sheets and uniformly distributed Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Moreover, the current work demonstrated a facile strategy to synthesize a Fe3O4@graphite sheet (direct one-pot synthesis) which economic approaches to greatly promote the electrocatalytic performance of Fe3O4@graphite sheets for water splitting.

Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel Edison: Methodology, Resources, Validation. Suguna Perumal: Writing - review & editing. Rajangam Vinodh: Formal analysis, Validation. Nallal Muthuchamy: Formal analysis, Software. Yong Rok Lee: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korean funded by the Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675), the Ministry of Science, Information and Communications Technology (MSIT) (2017R1C1B5076345) and the MSIT (2018R1A2B2004432).
